subtopics.title: Introduction to Vocabulary – Synonyms, Antonyms, Idioms

blocks.title: Why Vocabulary Matters
blocks.content: A strong vocabulary is essential for comprehension, writing, and competitive exams. Synonyms and antonyms test word relationships; idioms and phrases test cultural and contextual understanding.

blocks.title: Key Definitions
blocks.content:
• Synonym: A word with the same or similar meaning (e.g., happy → joyful).
• Antonym: A word with the opposite meaning (e.g., happy → sad).
• Idiom: A fixed expression with a figurative meaning (e.g., 'kick the bucket' = die).
• Phrase: A group of words that function as a unit (e.g., 'in a nutshell' = briefly).

blocks.title: How to Build Vocabulary
steps: Step 2: Learn root words, prefixes, and suffixes.
Step 3: Maintain a vocabulary journal with example sentences.
Step 4: Practice with flashcards and word lists.
Step 5: Apply new words in writing and speaking.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Synonyms and antonyms test word relationships.
Idioms have figurative meanings (not literal).
Phrases function as single units in a sentence.
Consistent reading and practice is the best way to build vocabulary.

subtopics.title: Types of Synonyms – Exact, Near, Contextual

blocks.title: Exact Synonyms
blocks.content: Words that are interchangeable in almost all contexts with the same meaning. Example: 'begin' and 'start'.

blocks.title: Near Synonyms
blocks.content: Words that share a general meaning but differ in nuance, formality, or usage. Example: 'happy' and 'content' – both mean positive emotion, but 'content' implies satisfaction.

blocks.title: Contextual Synonyms
blocks.content: Words that are synonyms only in specific contexts. Example: 'run' can be a synonym of 'manage' (run a company) or 'operate' (run a machine).

blocks.title: Examples of Synonym Types
rows: Depends on the noun being acted upon.

blocks.title: Example: Choosing the Right Synonym
blocks.find: Which of these is the closest synonym: plentiful, scarce, enough, overflowing?
steps: 'Overflowing' implies excess, which is closer to abundant.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Exact synonyms are interchangeable in all contexts.
Near synonyms have nuanced differences.
Contextual synonyms depend on the sentence context.
When choosing a synonym, always consider the context and intensity.

subtopics.title: Types of Antonyms – Complementary, Gradable, Relational

blocks.title: Complementary Antonyms
blocks.content: A binary pair where one excludes the other (A or Not A). Example: alive/dead, true/false, male/female. There is no middle ground.

blocks.title: Gradable Antonyms
blocks.content: Words that exist on a spectrum with degrees in between. Example: hot/cold (warm, cool, lukewarm), big/small (huge, large, tiny).

blocks.title: Relational Antonyms
blocks.content: Pairs that have a reciprocal relationship – one implies the other. Example: teacher/student, parent/child, buy/sell.

blocks.title: Examples of Antonym Types
rows: He is either alive or dead – not both.
The statement is either true or false.
The weather is hot, warm, cool, or cold.
The elephant is big; the ant is small.
If there is a teacher, there must be a student.
One person buys; the other sells.

blocks.title: Example: Identifying Antonym Type
blocks.find: What type of antonym is this?
steps: Employer and employee have a reciprocal relationship.
One cannot exist without the other.
blocks.formula_used: Check if one implies the other.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Complementary: No middle ground (alive/dead).
Gradable: Degrees in between (hot/cold).
Relational: One implies the other (teacher/student).
Identifying the type helps in understanding word relationships.

subtopics.title: Building Vocabulary – Root Words, Prefixes, and Suffixes

blocks.title: Root Words, Prefixes, and Suffixes
blocks.subtitle: The Building Blocks of English Vocabulary

blocks.title: Why Root Words Work
blocks.content: Over 60% of English words come from Latin and Greek roots. Learning roots helps you decode the meaning of unfamiliar words in exams.

blocks.title: Common Root Words and Their Meanings
Example: Using Root Words to Decode Meaning
Key Points
points: Root words carry the core meaning.
Prefixes modify meaning (e.g., un- = not).
Suffixes indicate part of speech or function.
Learning 100 roots can unlock thousands of words.

subtopics.title: Advanced Synonyms List – Commonly Tested in Exams

blocks.title: Memory Trick
blocks.memory_trick: Always Build Connections – link new words to words you already know.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Study synonyms in groups (e.g., intensity levels).
Pay attention to nuance (e.g., happy vs content vs ecstatic).
Use flashcards or apps to practice.
In exams, the closest synonym is often the most specific.

subtopics.title: Advanced Antonyms List – Commonly Tested in Exams

blocks.title: Trap: Choosing a Synonym instead of an Antonym.
blocks.wrong: If asked for an antonym of 'taciturn', selecting 'reserved'.
blocks.tip: Always read the question carefully – it will say 'synonym' or 'antonym'.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Antonyms test opposite relationships.
Some words have multiple antonyms depending on context.
Complementary antonyms have no middle ground.
Gradable antonyms have degrees.

subtopics.title: Introduction to Idioms – Definition and Usage

blocks.title: What is an Idiom?
blocks.content: An idiom is a fixed expression with a figurative meaning that is different from the literal meaning of its individual words. Example: 'It's raining cats and dogs' means 'it's raining heavily'.

blocks.title: Characteristics of Idioms
blocks.content:
• Fixed structure (cannot change words).
• Figurative meaning (not literal).
• Culturally specific.
• Often used in informal speech.

rows.left: Cannot be changed word by word.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Idioms have figurative meanings.
They are fixed expressions.
Understanding idioms is essential for comprehension and writing.
In exams, idioms are often tested through fill-in-the-blanks or synonym/antonym of the idiom's meaning.

subtopics.title: Common Idioms with Meanings and Examples

blocks.title: Common Idioms (A-I)
rows: Something that seems bad but turns out good.
Losing that job was a blessing in disguise.
Cheap phones are a dime a dozen.
The test was a piece of cake.
I had to bite the bullet and apologize.
Start a conversation in a social setting.
He told a joke to break the ice.
She burned the midnight oil to finish the report.
By the skin of your teeth
I passed the exam by the skin of my teeth.
Cost an arm and a leg
That car cost an arm and a leg.
Do something poorly to save time/money.
One who argues for the sake of argument.
He played devil's advocate to challenge the team.
Don't regret what can't be undone.
It's done – don't cry over spilt milk.
A very small amount compared to the whole.
His donation was a drop in the bucket.
Every cloud has a silver lining
Every bad situation has a positive side.
Every cloud has a silver lining – he found a better job.
I'll get the ball rolling with the introduction.
I'm tired – time to hit the sack.
In the blink of an eye
The accident happened in the blink of an eye.

blocks.title: Common Idioms (J-Z)
rows: Everyone jumped on the bandwagon to support the team.
Kill two birds with one stone
Achieve two things with one action.
I killed two birds with one stone by shopping while walking.
Let the cat out of the bag
She let the cat out of the bag about the surprise party.
Make a mountain out of a molehill
Stop making a mountain out of a molehill.
I missed the boat on buying that stock.
He visits us once in a blue moon.
The exam was a piece of cake.
Don't take it seriously – he's just pulling your leg.
He sat on the fence during the debate.
Who spilled the beans about the surprise?
The ball is in your court
It's your turn to make a decision.
I've done my part – the ball is in your court.
I'm a bit under the weather today.
Our plans are still up in the air.
He'll be on time when pigs fly.
You can't judge a book by its cover
He may look rough, but you can't judge a book by its cover.

blocks.title: Example: Idiom in Context
given: The new manager was a breath of fresh air for the team.
blocks.find: What is the meaning of 'a breath of fresh air'?
steps: Figurative meaning: someone or something new that brings positive change.
Answer: A refreshing and positive change.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Idioms are fixed expressions with figurative meanings.
Their meanings cannot be deduced from individual words.
Memorize the most common ones for exams.
Practice using them in sentences to retain meaning.

blocks.title: What are Phrases?
blocks.content: A phrase is a group of words that function as a single unit in a sentence. They can be literal or idiomatic and are often used in daily conversation.

blocks.title: Common Phrases with Meanings
rows: In a nutshell, the plan failed.
At the drop of a hat
He'll help at the drop of a hat.
By and large, the project was successful.
In the long run, it will save money.
On the other hand, we could save money.
He arrived out of the blue.
To make matters worse, it started raining.
We must save them at all costs.
He arrived in the nick of time.
The meeting started at 10 am on the dot.
The printer is out of order.
To sum up, we need more time.
Doing what one is good at.
She is in her element on stage.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Phrases are groups of words that function as units.
They can be literal or figurative.
Phrases are flexible and can be modified.
In exams, phrases are often tested in fill-in-the-blanks.

blocks.title: Tip 1 – Group by Theme
blocks.content: Group idioms by topic (e.g., weather: 'under the weather', 'rain cats and dogs'; animals: 'let the cat out of the bag', 'kill two birds with one stone').

blocks.title: Tip 2 – Learn with Stories
blocks.content: Create a short story using 5-10 idioms. This helps with recall.

blocks.title: Tip 3 – Use Flashcards
blocks.content: Use apps like Anki or Quizlet for spaced repetition.

blocks.title: Memory Trick
blocks.memory_trick: Sentences, Themes, Origins, Reading, Yell – methods to remember idioms.

blocks.title: Shortcut Summary
points: Group idioms by theme for easier recall.
Create stories with multiple idioms.
Use flashcards for spaced repetition.
Practice with example sentences.

blocks.title: Exam Strategy
blocks.content: Read the question carefully. Identify whether it asks for a synonym or antonym. For idioms, understand the context before choosing the meaning.

blocks.question: Choose the synonym of 'TENACIOUS'.
blocks.explanation: 'Tenacious' means persistent or determined. 'Weak' and 'lazy' are antonyms; 'fearful' is unrelated.

blocks.question: Choose the antonym of 'EPHEMERAL'.
blocks.explanation: 'Ephemeral' means short-lived. 'Permanent' is its antonym. 'Temporary' and 'fleeting' are synonyms.

blocks.question: What is the meaning of the idiom 'Bite the bullet'?
options: To face a difficult situation bravely
blocks.explanation: 'Bite the bullet' means to face a difficult or unpleasant situation with courage.

blocks.question: Choose the synonym of 'BENEVOLENT'.
blocks.explanation: 'Benevolent' means kind and charitable. 'Cruel', 'selfish', and 'indifferent' are antonyms.

blocks.question: Choose the antonym of 'CANDID'.
blocks.explanation: 'Candid' means honest and frank. 'Evasive' (avoiding direct answers) is its antonym.

blocks.question: What is the meaning of the phrase 'By and large'?
blocks.explanation: 'By and large' means generally or on the whole.

blocks.question: What is the meaning of the idiom 'A piece of cake'?
blocks.explanation: 'A piece of cake' means something very easy to do.

blocks.question: Choose the synonym of 'GREGARIOUS'.
blocks.explanation: 'Gregarious' means sociable and outgoing. 'Shy', 'reserved', and 'unfriendly' are antonyms.

blocks.question: Choose the antonym of 'PRAGMATIC'.
blocks.explanation: 'Pragmatic' means practical and realistic. 'Idealistic' is its antonym.

blocks.question: What is the meaning of the idiom 'Let the cat out of the bag'?
blocks.explanation: 'Let the cat out of the bag' means to reveal a secret or surprise.

blocks.title: Post-Test Review
blocks.content: Review the root words, prefixes, and suffixes. Practice with idiom groups. Use flashcards for spaced repetition.

blocks.title: Final Revision – Vocabulary
points: Synonyms are words with similar meanings.
Antonyms are words with opposite meanings.
Idioms have figurative meanings (not literal).
Phrases are groups of words that function as units.
Root words help decode unfamiliar words.
Prefixes and suffixes modify root words.
Use grouping and storytelling to memorize idioms.
Practice with MCQs to improve speed.
